query: efficacy of tebentafusp in uveal melanoma

retrieved_docs.title: Treatment of Cutaneous Malignancies With Topical, Oral, and Injectable Medication -- Clinical Significance -- Tebentafusp
retrieved_docs.content: Tebentafusp, a bispecific T-cell receptor therapy, represents a significant advancement in the treatment of metastatic uveal melanoma, which is a rare and aggressive ocular malignancy with limited effective systemic treatment options. This agent is engineered to target glycoprotein 100 (gp100), which is a melanocyte-specific antigen expressed on uveal melanoma cells, thereby redirecting cytotoxic T cells to recognize and eliminate these cancer cells. [83]

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Melanoma -- Treatment / Management -- Enucleation
retrieved_docs.content: For metastatic uveal melanoma, tebentafusp-tebn, a bispecific glycoprotein 100 peptide-HLA-directed CD3 T-cell engager, increases overall survival. This drug functions as an immune mobilizing monoclonal T-cell receptor against cancer (ImmTAC) class of T-cell–directed therapy and specifically targets HLA-A*02:01-positive metastatic uveal melanoma. [15] [16] In addition, darovasertib, an inhibitor of protein kinase C, increases overall survival in patients with metastatic uveal melanoma. [17] Further development of specific immunotherapy for ocular melanoma may lead to an increased frequency of performing biopsies to help direct therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Melanoma -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic testing post-tumor removal aids in prognosis and surveillance, with specific chromosomal aberrations and genetic markers indicating metastatic risk. With the continual development of new specific targeted therapies such as tebentafusp-tebn, indicated for HLA-A*02:01-positive adults, and darovasertib, continued education along with effective interprofessional communication is crucial, allowing seamless information exchange and collaborative decision-making among the team members. By embracing these principles of skill, strategy, interprofessional communication, and care coordination, healthcare professionals can deliver patient-centered care, ultimately improving patient outcomes and enhancing team performance in managing ocular melanoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Treatment of Cutaneous Malignancies With Topical, Oral, and Injectable Medication -- Clinical Significance -- Tebentafusp
retrieved_docs.content: In the pivotal phase 3 IMCgp100-202 trial, tebentafusp demonstrated a substantial improvement in overall survival compared to standard therapies. Among HLA-A*02:01-positive patients with metastatic uveal melanoma, the 1-year overall survival rate was 73% with tebentafusp compared to 59% in the control group, which received investigator's choice of standard therapy (eg, pembrolizumab, ipilimumab, or dacarbazine). PFS at 6 months was 31% with tebentafusp compared to 19% in the control group. This survival benefit marks a breakthrough in a disease historically associated with poor outcomes and limited responses to systemic treatments. [83]

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Melanoma -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: After tumor removal, genetic testing of the tumor can be helpful for prognosis and post-treatment surveillance of patients with primary uveal melanoma. These chromosomal aberrations can be detected using karyotypes, fluorescent in situ hybridization, and single-nucleotide polymorphism array analysis. Mutations in the BAP1 gene are associated with a high risk of metastatic disease, whereas mutations in SF3B1 and EIF1AX are associated with a more favorable prognosis and late onset of metastatic disease. In addition, patients with primary uveal melanoma should undergo a genotyping assay on whole blood for the presence of human leukocyte antigen (HLA)-A*02:01. Patients with this genotype are eligible for targeted therapy with tebentafusp-tebn.

retrieved_docs.title: Treatment of Cutaneous Malignancies With Topical, Oral, and Injectable Medication -- Clinical Significance -- Tebentafusp
retrieved_docs.content: Tebentafusp is associated with notable adverse effects, primarily immune-related toxicities. The most common adverse effects include cytokine release syndrome, which manifests as fever, hypotension, and fatigue, as well as dermatological-related reactions such as rash and pruritus. Despite these toxicities, most adverse effects are manageable with supportive care, and the significant survival benefit supports its continued use in this patient population. [83]

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Melanoma -- Treatment / Management -- Systemic Surveillance
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with high-risk tumors undergo systemic imaging every 3 to 6 months for the first 5 years, followed by every 6 to 12 months for years 6 to 12, and then as clinically indicated. MRI is the preferred diagnostic study for systemic imaging. CT scan and ultrasound are acceptable alternatives for patients with low-risk tumors.

retrieved_docs.content: BRAF Inhibitors Vemurafenib Dabrafenib MEK Inhibitors Trametinib BRAF and MEK Inhibitor Combinations Dabrafenib + Trametinib Vemurafenib + Cobimetinib

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Melanoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment of ocular melanoma depends on tumor size and location. The treatment options for patients with uveal melanoma include observation, plaque radiation therapy (RT), particle beam radiotherapy, transpupillary thermotherapy, laser photocoagulation, local surgical resection, and enucleation.

retrieved_docs.title: Vemurafenib -- Contraindications -- Warning and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Photosensitivity: Vemurafenib may cause mild-to-severe photosensitivity reactions. Patients should be advised to minimize sun exposure, wear protective clothing, and apply broad-spectrum sunscreen with SPF 30 or above. If photosensitivity becomes intolerable, dosage adjustment may be necessary. Ophthalmologic reactions: Uveitis and blurred vision have been reported in patients receiving vemurafenib. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Melanoma -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The 5-year survival rate for conjunctival melanoma with treatment is 83% to 84%, and the 10-year survival rate is 69% to 80%. The overall mortality for choroidal melanoma and ciliary body melanoma is 30% to 50% within 10 years, primarily due to metastatic disease. The overall mortality rate for iris melanoma is 0% to 11%.

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Melanoma -- Treatment / Management -- Enucleation
retrieved_docs.content: Enucleation provides no significant survival benefits over plaque brachytherapy in most patients. [14] Surgeons typically reserve enucleation for patients with large tumors, marked extrascleral extension, neovascular glaucoma, extensive retinal detachment, poor visual potential, or for patients who prefer enucleation.

retrieved_docs.title: Retinoblastoma -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Intravitreal Melphalan for Retinoblastoma: A Phase II trial is investigating the safety and efficacy of adding intravitreal melphalan to standard chemotherapy for early treatment of retinoblastoma with vitreous seeds. This approach seeks to improve local tumor control while minimizing systemic toxicity. [132] ( Comprehensive Cancer Information )

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Eyelid Lesions -- Treatment / Management -- Melanoma
retrieved_docs.content: For patients presenting with metastatic disease, surgical excision is not curative. Dacarbazine, an imidazole carboxamide chemotherapeutic agent, has been the first drug therapy for metastatic melanoma since 2006. [86]

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Eyelid Lesions -- Treatment / Management -- Melanoma
retrieved_docs.content: Biologic immunotherapies (e.g., interleukin-2 and interferons), vaccination strategies, and adoptive cell therapy are all immunotherapies that have been employed to combat metastatic malignant melanoma. Most notably, immune checkpoint inhibitors (i.e., ipilimumab, nivolumab, and pembrolizumab) are the newest and fastest-growing area of investigation in treating metastatic melanoma. Their discussion should be reserved for a separate article. [87] [88]

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Melanoma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Although localized interventions successfully deter relapse, nearly half of individuals with uveal tumors encounter potential metastatic progression due to early micrometastatic occurrences. Ocular melanoma commonly disseminates hematogenously, with the liver being a primary target. The overall prognosis depends on factors such as tumor size, location, and histologic traits. Importantly, the selection of local treatment does not impact long-term survival outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1553
retrieved_docs.content: Antitumor substances comprise a group of chemotherapeutic agents that include daktinomycin, azacytidin, busulfan and melphalan. These drugs are used for their cytotoxic activity against malignant cells and are classified within the therapeutic class of antineoplastic agents. A reference to “human herpesvirus 1” as a therapeutic class for antitumor substances is inconsistent with standard pharmacology, as antineoplastic agents are not primarily indicated for viral infections. Likewise, the identification of antitumor substances as “is a” dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate or prednisolone tebutate reflects a misclassification; dexamethasone phosphate and the prednisolone derivatives are corticosteroids that serve anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive roles rather than direct antitumor activity. Correctly distinguishing these categories clarifies that daktinomycin, azacytidin, busulfan and melphalan belong to the antitumor drug family, while dexamethasone phosphate and the prednisolone compounds belong to the corticosteroid therapeutic class. This separation is essential for accurate prescribing, patient counseling, and understanding of each drug’s mechanism of action.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 53

Collectively, these relationships demonstrate that antitumor agents span traditional cytotoxic drugs, corticosteroids, targeted inhibitors, and emerging viral therapies, reflecting the multifaceted strategies employed in contemporary cancer treatment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1729
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, potent (group III) represent a class of high‑potency corticosteroid agents, and amcinonide, budesonid, fluocinolonacetonid and fluocinonid are all members of this group. These four compounds share the pharmacologic characteristics of potent group III corticosteroids, which are commonly employed for their strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects in the treatment of dermatologic and other inflammatory conditions. Recognizing that amcinonide, budesonid, fluocinolonacetonid and fluocinonid belong to the same potent corticosteroid class helps clinicians select appropriate therapy and anticipate similar efficacy and safety profiles across these agents.

retrieved_docs.content: Amcinonide, beklomethason, and flutikason are all classified as potent group III corticosteroids. As members of this high‑potency corticosteroid class, each drug possesses strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties that are leveraged in the treatment of various dermatologic and inflammatory conditions. Their designation as group III agents indicates a higher potency compared with lower‑strength corticosteroid groups, which guides clinicians in selecting appropriate dosing regimens and monitoring for potential side‑effects associated with potent steroid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2862
retrieved_docs.content: Imidazole and triazole derivatives, topical antifungals represent a pharmacologic class of antifungal agents that includes the specific drugs isoconazole, omoconazole, and tioconazole. All three compounds share the imidazole or triazole chemical scaffold and are formulated for topical use to treat superficial fungal infections. Their classification within the same group highlights their similar mechanisms of action, spectrum of activity, and therapeutic applications in dermatologic antifungal therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: Amcinonide and fluocinonid are classified as members of the potent (group III) corticosteroids, a high‑potency subclass of corticosteroid agents. This classification indicates that both amcinonide and fluocinonid share the pharmacologic characteristics of potent group III corticosteroids, including strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects that are commonly utilized in dermatologic therapy for inflammatory skin conditions. Recognizing amcinonide and fluocinonid as members of this potent corticosteroid group helps clinicians select appropriate potency levels, balance therapeutic efficacy with safety, and guide appropriate short‑term use to limit systemic exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1737
retrieved_docs.content: Amcinonide and flutikason are both classified as members of the potent (group III) corticosteroid class, indicating that they belong to the high‑potency subclass of corticosteroid medications. As potent group III corticosteroids, they share strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are typically employed in clinical settings where a high‑potency topical steroid is required. Their shared classification guides clinicians in dosing, safety monitoring, and therapeutic decision‑making for conditions that benefit from potent corticosteroid treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1730
retrieved_docs.content: Amcinonide, flutikason, fluocinolonacetonid, and fluocinonid are all classified as members of the potent (group III) corticosteroid category. As high‑potency topical corticosteroids, these agents share strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are commonly used to treat dermatologic conditions that require potent local therapy. Recognizing that these four drugs belong to the same potency group assists clinicians in selecting appropriate dosing regimens, anticipating similar efficacy, and monitoring for comparable safety concerns such as skin atrophy with prolonged use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2494
retrieved_docs.content: Triazole derivatives, systemic antimycotics constitute a class of antifungal agents that are administered systemically to treat invasive fungal infections. Both itraconazole and voriconazole are members of this triazole derivative class, sharing the characteristic triazole ring structure that confers broad-spectrum activity against a variety of pathogenic fungi. Their classification as systemic antimycotics underscores their use in managing serious fungal diseases where topical therapy is insufficient, highlighting the importance of these agents in comprehensive antifungal treatment strategies.
